title: What does 'authentic' restoration mean?
summary: Heritage architecture has at least four mutually incompatible definitions of 'authenticity', and most public arguments about cathedrals, town halls and historic streets quietly collapse different definitions together. An essay on why the disagreement matters.
body:
Whenever a fire or an earthquake damages a much-loved building — Notre-Dame in Paris, the Old Bridge in Mostar, the Glasgow School of Art — there is, predictably, a public debate about how to rebuild. The debate has, on its surface, the appearance of a single question: should the building be restored to look as it did before? In practice, the debate is not one question but several, layered on top of each other, and most of the disagreement that follows can be traced to a quiet refusal — by all sides — to admit which question is actually being argued.

For purposes of this essay it is useful to distinguish at least four definitions of 'authenticity', each of which has a long and respectable pedigree in conservation theory. The first is material authenticity: the building is 'authentic' insofar as it consists of the original stones, timber, plaster, and glass placed there by the original builders. By this definition, a wall rebuilt from new stones cut from the same quarry is not authentic, however convincingly it imitates the original. The second is formal authenticity: the building is authentic insofar as its current shape, proportions, surfaces and silhouettes match what was there before. By this definition, a wall rebuilt in indistinguishable new materials is fully authentic; what matters is the form, not the substance.

The third is functional authenticity, less often discussed but no less important. A medieval cathedral was built to be prayed in, not photographed; a Renaissance palace was built to be lived in; a nineteenth-century arcade was built to be walked through on a wet afternoon. A restoration that returns the building to its original use, even imperfectly, has — on this view — preserved something that an empty museum-quality reconstruction has not. The fourth is what we might call genealogical authenticity: the building is authentic because it has, through every period of its history, been continually cared for, repaired, modified, and adapted by the community that lived around it. On this definition, a sympathetic eighteenth-century alteration is part of the building's authenticity, not an imposition upon it.

These four definitions are not merely different. They are, in many practical cases, mutually incompatible. After the 1993 destruction of Mostar's Old Bridge, the international team that rebuilt it was widely praised for using original-quarry stone in the original techniques. Material authenticity, in the limited sense available, was protected. But the Mostar that walks across the new bridge is not the Mostar of 1993; the ethnic-cleansing that preceded the bridge's destruction tore apart, irreversibly, the genealogical authenticity in which a single community would, over generations, have repaired its own crossing. The new stones are real, in the sense the conservators intended; the community of repair is not. Both versions of authenticity have been honoured and betrayed in the same act.

The Notre-Dame debate after 2019 followed a similar pattern. Modernists argued that an entirely new spire would honour the cathedral's long history of additions (a genealogical argument). Traditionalists argued that the rebuilt spire should match Viollet-le-Duc's nineteenth-century reconstruction (a formal argument). Almost no one defended the practically lost option of finding the original twelfth-century roof timber, which had simply burned away (a material argument). Functional authenticity — the question of whether the cathedral remained a working church — was largely assumed and therefore not discussed. The debate sounded heated because all four definitions were in the room at once, none of them named.

What follows from acknowledging the distinction? Two things, modestly. First, public consultations about heritage damage would be better conducted if participants were asked, at the outset, which definition of authenticity they wished to protect, and why. Second, the conservator's craft — already a thoughtful one — would be slightly relieved of the burden of pretending that 'authenticity' is a single, recoverable value. It is not. It is four overlapping and often-conflicting values that can sometimes all be partially honoured, never all fully so.

None of this resolves any specific case. The point of the distinction is not to deliver verdicts but to clear away false ones. An honest decision about a damaged cathedral will name which authenticity it is privileging, will acknowledge what it is therefore sacrificing, and will publish that acknowledgement alongside the work. Most decisions of this kind, for understandable reasons of public relations, do not. The result is that we agree on the choice and continue to argue, inconclusively, about its meaning. This essay is one small attempt to interrupt that cycle.

annotations.meaningVi: có nguồn gốc, lịch sử học thuật đáng kính
hình bóng / đường viền (của kiến trúc)
thuộc về dòng dõi / truyền nối qua các thế hệ
sự áp đặt từ bên ngoài
kiến trúc sư người Pháp thế kỷ 19, trùng tu nhiều công trình Gothic
phán quyết / kết luận cuối cùng

grammarNotes.title: Hedged taxonomies: 'at least four', 'in many practical cases', 'not merely different'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Văn nghị luận C1 thường vừa đưa ra phân loại vừa lùi một bước để khỏi tỏ ra dứt khoát. Trong bài: 'at least four definitions of "authenticity"' (mở để có thêm). 'in many practical cases, mutually incompatible' (không khẳng định 'mọi') . 'less often discussed but no less important' (chèn so sánh thừa nhận). 'in the limited sense available' (hạn chế phạm vi của 'material authenticity'). 'practically lost' (thừa nhận khả năng tồn tại lý thuyết). Các cụm này cho phép tác giả phân loại mạnh mẽ mà vẫn để chỗ cho ngoại lệ — đặc trưng của diễn ngôn học thuật C1.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: C1 argumentation often offers a taxonomy and simultaneously steps back from it. The essay uses 'at least four definitions of "authenticity"' (open to more). 'In many practical cases, mutually incompatible' (not all cases). 'Less often discussed but no less important' (parenthetical concession). 'In the limited sense available' (qualifies material authenticity). 'Practically lost' (admits a theoretical possibility). These hedges let a writer make a firm categorisation while preserving room for edge cases — characteristic of academic-register C1.

translationVi:
Mỗi khi một vụ cháy hoặc động đất làm hỏng một công trình được yêu mến — Notre-Dame ở Paris, Cầu Cũ ở Mostar, Trường Mỹ thuật Glasgow — đều có, đoán trước được, một cuộc tranh luận công khai về cách phục dựng. Bề mặt, tranh luận có vẻ là một câu hỏi duy nhất: có nên phục dựng để công trình trông như trước hay không? Thực tế, tranh luận không phải một câu hỏi mà là nhiều câu xếp chồng lên nhau, và phần lớn bất đồng kéo theo có thể truy về sự từ chối lặng lẽ — của mọi phía — thừa nhận câu nào thật sự đang được tranh luận.

Vì mục đích của bài này, cần phân biệt ít nhất bốn định nghĩa về 'authenticity' (tính nguyên bản), mỗi cái đều có lịch sử và truyền thống đáng nể trong lý thuyết bảo tồn. Thứ nhất là tính nguyên bản vật liệu: công trình 'nguyên bản' nếu nó còn chứa đúng đá, gỗ, vữa và kính mà người xây ban đầu đặt vào đó. Theo định nghĩa này, một bức tường được xây lại bằng đá mới khai thác từ chính mỏ đá ấy thì không nguyên bản, dù bắt chước rất khéo. Thứ hai là tính nguyên bản hình thức: công trình nguyên bản nếu hình dáng hiện tại, tỉ lệ, bề mặt và đường viền khớp với trước đây. Theo định nghĩa này, một bức tường xây bằng vật liệu mới không phân biệt được vẫn nguyên bản hoàn toàn; cái quan trọng là hình thức, không phải chất liệu.

Thứ ba là tính nguyên bản chức năng, ít được bàn hơn nhưng không kém quan trọng. Một nhà thờ trung cổ được xây để cầu nguyện, không phải để chụp ảnh; một cung điện Phục Hưng được xây để có người ở; một khu mua sắm thế kỷ XIX được xây để dạo bộ vào một chiều mưa. Một sự phục dựng đưa công trình về công năng gốc của nó, dù không hoàn hảo, đã — theo quan điểm này — bảo tồn được điều mà một sự tái dựng kiểu bảo tàng trống rỗng đã không. Thứ tư là cái có thể gọi là tính nguyên bản phả hệ: công trình nguyên bản vì nó, qua từng thời kỳ lịch sử, đã được cộng đồng quanh nó chăm sóc, sửa chữa, điều chỉnh và thích nghi liên tục. Theo định nghĩa này, một sửa chữa thiện chí thế kỷ XVIII là một phần của tính nguyên bản công trình, không phải sự áp đặt lên nó.

Bốn định nghĩa này không chỉ khác nhau. Trong nhiều trường hợp thực tế, chúng còn không tương thích. Sau khi Cầu Cũ Mostar bị phá huỷ năm 1993, đội quốc tế phục dựng nó được khen rộng rãi vì dùng đá từ đúng mỏ gốc với kỹ thuật gốc. Tính nguyên bản vật liệu, theo nghĩa giới hạn còn lại được, đã được bảo vệ. Nhưng Mostar đi qua cây cầu mới không phải Mostar của năm 1993; sự thanh trừng sắc tộc trước khi cầu bị phá đã xé nát, không thể hồi phục, tính nguyên bản phả hệ trong đó một cộng đồng duy nhất, qua nhiều thế hệ, đáng lẽ sẽ tự tu sửa cây cầu của mình. Đá mới là thật, theo nghĩa các nhà bảo tồn muốn; cộng đồng sửa chữa thì không. Cả hai phiên bản nguyên bản đều được tôn vinh và bị phản bội trong cùng một hành động.

Cuộc tranh luận về Notre-Dame sau 2019 đi theo dạng tương tự. Phe hiện đại lập luận rằng một mái nhọn hoàn toàn mới sẽ tôn vinh lịch sử bồi đắp của nhà thờ (lập luận phả hệ). Phe truyền thống lập luận rằng mái nhọn phục dựng nên khớp với phiên bản Viollet-le-Duc thế kỷ XIX (lập luận hình thức). Gần như không ai bảo vệ phương án thực tế đã mất là tìm lại được phần gỗ mái thế kỷ XII đã đơn giản cháy mất (lập luận vật liệu). Tính nguyên bản chức năng — câu hỏi liệu nhà thờ có vẫn là nhà thờ hoạt động không — phần lớn được mặc nhiên và do đó không bàn. Tranh luận nghe rất căng vì cả bốn định nghĩa cùng có mặt trong phòng, không cái nào được gọi tên.

Vậy theo sau sự phân biệt này là gì? Hai điều, khiêm tốn. Thứ nhất, các phiên tham vấn công về hư hại di sản sẽ tốt hơn nếu, từ đầu, người tham gia được hỏi mình muốn bảo vệ định nghĩa nguyên bản nào, và vì sao. Thứ hai, nghề bảo tồn — vốn đã đầy suy tư — sẽ được trút bỏ phần nào gánh nặng phải giả vờ rằng 'nguyên bản' là một giá trị đơn nhất và có thể phục hồi. Không phải. Đó là bốn giá trị chồng lấn và xung đột, đôi khi có thể tôn vinh một phần cả bốn, không bao giờ tôn vinh hoàn toàn cả bốn.

Không điều nào trong số này giải quyết bất kỳ trường hợp cụ thể nào. Mục đích của sự phân biệt không phải để ra phán quyết mà để dọn dẹp những phán quyết sai. Một quyết định trung thực với một nhà thờ bị hư sẽ gọi tên định nghĩa nguyên bản nào nó đang ưu tiên, sẽ thừa nhận do đó nó đang hy sinh điều gì, và sẽ công bố lời thừa nhận đó cùng với công trình. Phần lớn quyết định kiểu này, vì lý do quan hệ công chúng dễ hiểu, không làm vậy. Kết quả là chúng ta đồng ý về lựa chọn và tiếp tục cãi nhau, không kết luận, về ý nghĩa của nó. Bài này là một nỗ lực nhỏ để cắt đứt vòng đó.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's diagnosis of the apparent single 'should-we-rebuild' question?
options: It is in fact several layered questions, and most disagreement comes from a refusal to admit which one is actually being argued.
It is a simple yes-or-no question.
It is only a financial question.
It is best left to architects.

questions.prompt: Define MATERIAL authenticity, as the writer uses it.
options: The building consists of the original stones, timber, plaster and glass placed by the original builders.
The building looks the way it used to.
The building is still used for its original purpose.
The community has continuously cared for it.
The current shape, proportions, surfaces and silhouettes match what was there before — substance does not matter, form does.
Returning the building to its original use — a cathedral prayed in, a palace lived in, an arcade walked through.
Continual care, repair, modification, and adaptation by the community that lived around the building over time.
Strict use of the very first version only.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about the relationship between the four definitions in practice?
options: They are not merely different — they are, in many practical cases, mutually incompatible.
Three are correct, one is false.
They reduce to a single principle.
questions.explanation: 'They are not merely different. They are, in many practical cases, mutually incompatible.'

questions.prompt: What does the Mostar Bridge case illustrate, according to the writer?
options: Material authenticity was honoured (original-quarry stone), but the genealogical authenticity (the community of repair, torn apart by ethnic cleansing) was not — both honoured and betrayed in the same act.
The bridge should not have been rebuilt.

questions.prompt: Which authenticity did the modernists in the Notre-Dame debate appeal to?
options: Genealogical — a new spire would honour the cathedral's long history of additions.

questions.prompt: Which authenticity did the traditionalists appeal to?
Why does the writer say the Notre-Dame debate 'sounded heated'?
options: All four definitions were in the room at once, none of them named.

questions.prompt: What two modest consequences does the writer draw from his distinction?
options: Public consultations should start by asking which authenticity participants want to protect; the conservator's craft is relieved of pretending 'authenticity' is a single recoverable value.
Buildings should never be rebuilt; all decisions should be made by architects.
Heritage should be privatised; tourists should be banned.
Cathedrals should be replaced by modern halls; communities should be silent.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's view of 'authenticity' as a value?
options: It is four overlapping and often-conflicting values; they can sometimes all be partially honoured, never all fully.
It depends entirely on the architect.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about specific verdicts?
options: His point is not to deliver verdicts but to clear away false ones; honest decisions name which authenticity they privilege and publish that alongside the work.
He gives a verdict on Notre-Dame.
He says all decisions should be made by referendum.
Verdicts should be hidden from the public.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the essay NOT claiming?
options: That one definition of authenticity is objectively correct and the others are wrong.
That the four definitions are often mutually incompatible in practice.
That genealogical authenticity can be destroyed independently of material authenticity.
That an honest decision should publish its acknowledgements alongside the work.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly refuses to pick a winner among the four.
questions.hint: Look for the option the writer rejects throughout.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best summarises the writer's framing of 'authenticity'?
options: 'Four overlapping and often-conflicting values that can sometimes all be partially honoured, never all fully so.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: Heritage debates are unproductive when participants treat 'authenticity' as a single value; naming which of four definitions one is privileging — and what is being sacrificed — would make decisions more honest and disagreements more productive.
Heritage buildings should not be rebuilt.
questions.explanation: The essay's purpose is exactly this clarification.
questions.hint: Notice that the writer refuses to pick a side and instead reframes the debate.

cloze.template:
Whenever a fire or an earthquake damages a much-loved building — Notre-Dame in Paris, the Old Bridge in Mostar, the Glasgow School of Art — there is, predictably, a public {{b1}} about how to rebuild. The debate has, on its surface, the appearance of a single question: should the building be restored to look as it did before? In practice, the debate is not one question but several, layered on top of each other, and most of the disagreement that follows can be traced to a quiet refusal — by all sides — to admit which question is actually being {{b2}}.

For purposes of this essay it is useful to distinguish at least four definitions of '{{b3}}', each of which has a long and respectable pedigree in conservation theory. The first is material authenticity: the building is 'authentic' insofar as it consists of the original {{b4}}, timber, plaster, and glass placed there by the original builders. By this definition, a wall rebuilt from new stones cut from the same quarry is not authentic, however convincingly it imitates the original. The second is formal authenticity: the building is authentic insofar as its current {{b5}}, proportions, surfaces and silhouettes match what was there before. By this definition, a wall rebuilt in indistinguishable new materials is fully authentic; what matters is the form, not the substance.

The third is {{b6}} authenticity, less often discussed but no less important. A medieval cathedral was built to be prayed in, not photographed; a Renaissance palace was built to be lived in; a nineteenth-century arcade was built to be walked through on a wet afternoon. A restoration that returns the building to its original use, even imperfectly, has — on this view — preserved something that an empty museum-quality reconstruction has not. The fourth is what we might call {{b7}} authenticity: the building is authentic because it has, through every period of its history, been continually cared for, repaired, modified, and adapted by the community that lived around it. On this definition, a sympathetic eighteenth-century alteration is part of the building's authenticity, not an {{b8}} upon it.

These four definitions are not merely different. They are, in many practical cases, mutually {{b9}}. After the 1993 destruction of Mostar's Old Bridge, the international team that rebuilt it was widely praised for using original-quarry stone in the original techniques. Material authenticity, in the limited sense available, was protected. But the Mostar that walks across the new bridge is not the Mostar of 1993; the {{b10}} that preceded the bridge's destruction tore apart, irreversibly, the genealogical authenticity in which a single community would, over generations, have repaired its own crossing. The new stones are real, in the sense the conservators intended; the {{b11}} of repair is not. Both versions of authenticity have been honoured and betrayed in the same act.

The Notre-Dame debate after 2019 followed a similar pattern. Modernists argued that an entirely new spire would honour the cathedral's long history of additions (a genealogical argument). Traditionalists argued that the rebuilt spire should match Viollet-le-Duc's nineteenth-century reconstruction (a formal argument). Functional authenticity — the question of whether the cathedral remained a working {{b12}} — was largely assumed and therefore not discussed. The debate sounded heated because all four definitions were in the room at once, none of them named.

What follows from acknowledging the distinction? Two things, modestly. First, public consultations about heritage damage would be better conducted if participants were asked, at the outset, which definition of authenticity they wished to {{b13}}, and why. Second, the conservator's craft — already a thoughtful one — would be slightly relieved of the burden of pretending that 'authenticity' is a single, recoverable value. It is not. It is four overlapping and often-conflicting {{b14}} that can sometimes all be partially honoured, never all fully so.

None of this resolves any specific case. The point of the distinction is not to deliver verdicts but to clear away false ones. An honest decision about a damaged cathedral will name which authenticity it is privileging, will acknowledge what it is therefore sacrificing, and will {{b15}} that acknowledgement alongside the work. Most decisions of this kind, for understandable reasons of public relations, do not. The result is that we agree on the choice and continue to argue, inconclusively, about its meaning. This essay is one small attempt to interrupt that cycle.

blanks.explanation: Which question is actually being argued.
The community of repair is not real now.
Which definition they wish to protect.
Publish the acknowledgement alongside the work.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay argues that calling 'authenticity' four different things at once is what makes heritage debates so heated. Is there a value-laden word you use at work, in your family, or in public life — 'fairness', 'safety', 'respect', 'tradition' — that you and your interlocutors are quietly using to mean different things? Which of its meanings are mutually incompatible, and what would happen if everyone had to declare which one they were defending today?
